steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The key principle is stated upfront: meaningful sounds are perceived as quieter than noise of the same volume. The researchers noted that participants may have treated the nature sounds as noise. The blank asks what a participant would have experienced when hearing Metallica's "Sad but True" and the nature sounds at the same volume (61.5 dB).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — experienced the nature sounds as louder than "Sad but True" even though they were not.: Music is meaningful, so it is perceived as quieter. Nature sounds were treated as noise, so they are not perceived as quieter. At equal actual volume, the nature sounds would seem louder. ✓
  • Choice B — believed that neither the nature sounds nor "Sad but True" were at a volume of 61.5 decibels.: The principle is about relative perceived loudness, not about whether participants can identify exact decibel levels. ✗
  • Choice C — perceived the nature sounds and "Sad but True" to be comparably meaningful despite perceiving the music to be quieter.: This contradicts the setup — the nature sounds were treated as noise (not meaningful), which is exactly why the perception differs. ✗
  • Choice D — adjusted the volume of the nature sounds to be greater than 61.5 decibels.: The question asks about perception, not about what volume adjustments participants made. ✗
A is correct.
